summary.title: Scenario 2 - Third-Party API Integration
summary.description: An e-commerce platform integrates with a third-party payment gateway to process customer transactions via REST API using API keys. Transaction results are stored in an internal database and displayed via the frontend. Order confirmation emails are sent through a third-party email service.

data.name: Public Internet
data.description: Untrusted external zone where the customer initiates purchase requests from their browser

data.name: Internal Application Zone
data.description: Trusted internal zone hosting the e-commerce frontend, backend, and transaction database. Separated from all external and third-party services.

data.name: Payment Gateway (Third Party)
data.description: External untrusted zone hosting the third-party payment gateway service. Communications cross a trust boundary and must be treated as potentially hostile.

data.name: Email Service (Third Party)
data.description: External untrusted zone hosting the third-party email delivery service used to send order confirmation emails to customers.

data.name: Customer
data.description: End user browsing the e-commerce platform and initiating a product purchase over HTTPS from the public internet

data.name: E-Commerce Frontend
data.description: Web frontend served to the customer browser. Collects payment details and purchase intent, then forwards requests to the backend over an internal connection.

threats.title: Cross-Site Scripting on Payment Page
threats.description: An attacker injects malicious scripts into the e-commerce frontend payment page through user-supplied input fields. When a victim customer visits the page, the script executes in their browser, stealing payment card details or session tokens before they are submitted to the backend.
threats.mitigation: Implement a strict Content Security Policy header. Sanitise and encode all user-supplied output server-side. Use HttpOnly and Secure cookie flags. Perform regular frontend security testing and code reviews.

data.name: E-Commerce Backend
data.description: Core application server that processes payment requests, communicates with the payment gateway via REST API using API keys, stores transaction results, and triggers email notifications.

threats.title: API Key Theft and Backend Impersonation
threats.description: If the payment gateway API key is exposed through insecure storage such as hardcoding in source code, environment variable leaks, or application logs, an attacker can impersonate the e-commerce backend and submit fraudulent payment requests or retrieve sensitive transaction data directly from the payment gateway.
threats.mitigation: Store API keys exclusively in a secrets management system such as HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager. Never hardcode credentials in source code or commit them to version control. Rotate API keys regularly. Restrict API key permissions to the minimum required operations.

threats.title: Payment Response Tampering
threats.description: An attacker performs a man-in-the-middle attack on the connection between the backend and the payment gateway, or compromises an internal proxy, to modify the payment response from a failed status to a success status. This allows fraudulent orders to be processed without actual payment being collected.
threats.mitigation: Enforce mutual TLS for all payment gateway communications. Validate payment gateway responses using webhook signatures or response MAC codes. Implement independent server-side payment verification by querying the gateway directly after receiving a response.

threats.title: Payment Gateway Request Flooding
threats.description: An attacker sends a high volume of forged or malformed payment requests to the backend, causing the backend to exhaust its API rate limit with the payment gateway or consume all available processing threads. This renders the checkout process unavailable for legitimate customers, resulting in revenue loss.
threats.mitigation: Implement per-user and per-IP rate limiting on the checkout endpoint. Add CAPTCHA verification before payment submission. Use a Web Application Firewall to detect and block request flooding patterns. Monitor payment API usage and alert on anomalous spikes.

data.name: Transaction Database
data.description: Internal relational database storing transaction records, payment statuses, customer order details, and audit logs

threats.title: Lack of Transaction Audit Trail
threats.description: Without immutable transaction logging, a customer could deny having placed an order or a fraudulent insider could modify transaction records to conceal unauthorised refunds or fraudulent charges. The absence of a verifiable audit trail makes it impossible to resolve disputes or detect tampering after the fact.
threats.mitigation: Implement append-only audit logging for all transaction events with cryptographic timestamps. Store logs in a separate write-protected data store. Include transaction IDs, amounts, timestamps, user identifiers, and IP addresses in every log entry. Retain logs for a minimum of seven years for compliance.

threats.title: Unauthorised Access to Transaction Records
threats.description: An attacker exploits insufficient access controls or SQL injection vulnerabilities in the backend to query or modify transaction records directly. This could allow a low-privileged user or external attacker to access other customers payment history, alter payment statuses, or issue unauthorised refunds.
threats.mitigation: Enforce role-based access control with least-privilege database accounts for each application component. Use parameterised queries for all database operations. Encrypt sensitive transaction fields at the application layer using AES-256. Conduct quarterly access control reviews and database penetration testing.

data.name: Payment Gateway
data.description: Third-party external payment processing service. Receives REST API payment requests from the backend, processes card charges, and returns transaction results.

threats.title: Sensitive Payment Data Exposure in Transit
threats.description: If TLS is misconfigured or a weak cipher suite is accepted on the connection between the backend and payment gateway, an attacker intercepting network traffic could expose customer payment card details, API keys, and transaction amounts as they cross the trust boundary between the internal zone and the third-party service.
threats.mitigation: Enforce TLS 1.2 or higher with strong cipher suites on all outbound payment gateway connections. Pin the payment gateway TLS certificate to detect substitution attacks. Never log full card numbers or API keys. Follow PCI-DSS requirements for cardholder data in transit.

data.name: Email Service
data.description: Third-party external email delivery service used to send order confirmation emails to customers after successful payment processing.

data.name: Purchase Request (HTTPS)
data.description: Customer submits purchase and payment details via the browser over HTTPS

data.name: Payment Request (Internal)
data.description: Frontend forwards payment details and order information to the backend for processing

data.name: Payment API Request (REST/HTTPS)
data.description: Backend sends payment request to the third-party payment gateway via REST API authenticated with an API key over HTTPS

data.name: Payment Result (REST/HTTPS)
data.description: Payment gateway returns transaction result including success or failure status and transaction reference ID

data.name: Store Transaction Record (SQL)
data.description: Backend writes transaction result, order details, and payment status to the internal transaction database

data.name: Transaction Confirmation
data.description: Database returns confirmation of stored transaction record to the backend

data.name: Send Confirmation Email (SMTP/API)
data.description: Backend sends order details and customer email address to the third-party email service to trigger an order confirmation email

data.name: Order Confirmation Response
data.description: Backend returns order confirmation and transaction status to the frontend for display to the customer

data.name: Order Success Page (HTTPS)
data.description: Frontend displays the order confirmation page to the customer over HTTPS
